serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of kibbeh is 3–4 fried balls (approximately 200 g), as commonly portioned on food delivery platforms in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Kibbeh (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for fried kibbeh balls as a composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for bulgur wheat (FDC ID 169707), ground lamb (FDC ID 174381), ground beef 80/20 (FDC ID 174032), pine nuts (FDC ID 170591), and frying oil absorption estimates consistent with USDA deep-frying methodology. The input record's per-100g values (energy 275 kcal, protein 14 g, fat 19 g, carbohydrate 12.5 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained. Secondary cross-reference: FAO/INFOODS Middle Eastern food composition data for kibbeh-type dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Bulgur Wheat and Mixed Meat Composite Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for fried kibbeh exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 48 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being bulgur wheat, which has a published GI of approximately 46–48 (University of Sydney GI Database, bulgur wheat boiled, GI=46; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The protein (14 g/100g) and fat (19 g/100g) content further attenuate glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 25 g) / 100 = 12.0. Serving carbohydrate = 12.5 g/100g × 200 g / 100 = 25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat 6 g/100g exceeds the AHA 5 g/100g threshold; sodium 225 mg/100g is borderline; a 200 g serving delivers 450 mg sodium and 12 g saturated fat.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit kibbeh to occasional consumption, request baked rather than deep-fried preparation to reduce saturated fat load, and monitor total daily sodium intake given the 450 mg per serving contribution.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 12.5 g/100g from bulgur wheat (low GI ~48); glycemic load per 200 g serving is 12 (moderate); saturated fat 6 g/100g may worsen insulin resistance with frequent consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kibbeh has a low GI due to bulgur wheat and high protein content, making it a moderate glycaemic choice for people with Type 2 Diabetes, but portion control is essential and deep-fried preparation should be replaced with baked to reduce saturated fat and caloric density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6 g/100g from red meat and frying oil; trans fat 0.25 g/100g; deep-frying generates oxidised lipids; a 200 g serving delivers 12 g saturated fat and 0.5 g trans fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fried kibbeh poses a high cardiovascular concern due to elevated saturated fat and trans fat from deep-frying with red meat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid the fried version and opt for baked kibbeh with lean meat substitutions to reduce atherogenic lipid exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); red meat (lamb and beef) is high in arachidonic acid amplifying TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; saturated fat 6 g/100g exceeds RA threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried kibbeh due to AGE formation and pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid from red meat; a baked version with chicken or turkey substitution and retention of pine kernels and cinnamon is preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kibbeh (also spelled kibbe, kubba, or kubbeh) is a traditional Levantine dish originating from Lebanon, Syria, Turkey, and Iraq, widely consumed across the Middle East and in diaspora communities globally. The dish consists of oval or round shells made from fine bulgur wheat mixed with minced lamb or beef and spices, stuffed with a filling of coarsely ground meat, sautéed onion, pine kernels, cinnamon, allspice, and cumin, then deep-fried until golden brown and crisp. The exterior has a firm, slightly crunchy texture from the bulgur crust, while the interior is moist and aromatic. Kibbeh is served as a snack, appetiser, or main dish, often accompanied by yoghurt dip, hummus, or fresh salad. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 275 kcal, 14 g protein, 19 g fat (6 g saturated), 12.5 g carbohydrate, 2 g dietary fibre, and 225 mg sodium. The bulgur wheat base gives kibbeh a lower glycaemic index (~48) than refined-grain alternatives, but deep-frying significantly increases total fat and saturated fat content. Clinically, the high saturated fat (6 g/100g) and trans fat (0.25 g/100g) from deep-frying make fried kibbeh a concern for cardiovascular disease and hypertension management. The dish is halal-certified in most commercial preparations and contains gluten (bulgur) and tree nuts (pine kernels) as allergens. In Singapore, kibbeh is available through Middle Eastern and Turkish food delivery outlets and is typically portioned as 3–4 balls (~200 g per serving).

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for fried kibbeh as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries (bulgur wheat, ground lamb, ground beef, pine nuts) and are consistent with the input record's values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using bulgur wheat as the dominant carbohydrate source. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries; no single exact entry for fried kibbeh exists in any approved primary source. Input record values retained as they are consistent with the composite estimate. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for fried kibbeh as a composite dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA database as of 2026-04-16. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (7.5) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.5) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for ground lamb, ground beef 80/20, and pine nuts weighted by approximate recipe proportions; these are estimates and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis. 4. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on bulgur wheat GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for fried kibbeh exists. 5. The input record listed primary_cuisine as Turkish and country_of_origin as Singapore; cuisine has been corrected to Middle Eastern / Levantine reflecting the dish's true origin; Singapore is retained as the market context. 6. Kibbeh is not a Singaporean dish; therefore the G6 multilingual rule for Singaporean dishes (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) does not apply. The dish is Levantine/Middle Eastern; Arabic alias (كبة) is included for completeness. 7. All aliases_by_language string values appear verbatim in the flat aliases array (q8 self-check passed).
